context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Urban-Rural Linkages and Tradeoffs

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of the urban bias thesis in development? Should countries focus more on linkages that strengthen urban or rural development? What's the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about the urban bias thesis, urban-rural linkages, and how industrial growth affects class formation and urbanization. I’m honestly not sure how to organize my argument yet—could you help me figure out a clear main claim first?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Before we talk structure, let's figure out what you actually think. What do you already know or believe about the urban bias thesis — what is it essentially arguing, and do you find it convincing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and asking for help forming a main claim. Rather than suggesting one, I should use Socratic questioning to get them to articulate what they already think about the urban bias thesis. This will help them generate their own argument organically. I'll ask a single focused question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: I think it’s basically saying governments tend to favor cities over rural areas in development policy, like more investment, services, and political attention go to urban groups. I do find that convincing, but I’m not sure if the argument is that this is always bad, or more that it creates uneven development and hurts rural people.
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful distinction you're drawing. So which do you think it is — is urban bias *inherently* bad, or is the real problem something more specific, like what it does to rural populations or to the relationship between the two? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid grasp of the basic urban bias thesis and is already identifying a nuance — that the issue may be about uneven development rather than urban investment being inherently bad. This is a good foundation. I should push them to take a position on this nuance, which will help them form their main claim naturally.

exchanges.student: My instinct is that it’s not inherently bad for states to invest in cities, because cities can drive growth, but it becomes a problem when that growth depends on neglecting rural areas. I think I’m leaning toward the idea that the bigger issue is the imbalance and the weak link between urban and rural development, not just “cities getting attention” by itself.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong instinct and a solid foundation for your argument. Now, can you try to put that into one or two sentences — a clear claim that could anchor your whole reflection? What exactly are you arguing should happen, or what's going wrong when that balance is missing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and nuanced position — urban investment isn't inherently bad, but the problem is imbalance and weak urban-rural linkages. This is essentially their main claim. I should affirm the direction and push them to sharpen it into a more precise thesis statement they can use in their paper, without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe my claim is that the urban bias thesis is useful because it shows how development often privileges cities, but the solution is not to shift all attention to rural areas instead. I think I’m arguing that countries should build stronger urban-rural linkages so industrial growth supports both, otherwise class inequality and uneven urbanization get worse.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-rounded claim that hits all three parts of the prompt. One part still feels underdeveloped though — how exactly does industrial growth worsen class inequality and uneven urbanization when those linkages are weak? What's the mechanism you have in mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid main claim that addresses all three parts of the prompt: the urban bias thesis, urban-rural linkages, and the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization. I should affirm this and push them to think about how industrial growth specifically connects to class formation and urbanization, since that's the part of their claim that still needs more development and is a key prompt requirement.

exchanges.student: I’m a little stuck on that part. I guess industrial growth can pull people into cities for jobs, but if rural areas stay underinvested, migration speeds up and cities get overcrowded? And maybe the benefits go mostly to factory owners, urban middle classes, or skilled workers while migrants end up in informal work, but I’m not sure if I’m saying that the right way.
exchanges.tutor: You're not wrong — those are real dynamics. Now try connecting them: how do those two things you just described (migration patterns and who captures the benefits) relate to each other? What does that tell us about how class formation happens in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but second-guessing themselves. They've identified two solid mechanisms: rural-urban migration driven by underinvestment, and unequal distribution of industrial gains leading to class stratification. I should validate the direction and push them to connect these two ideas more explicitly rather than treating them as separate observations.

exchanges.student: I think the connection is that industrial growth creates opportunity, but not evenly. It draws rural migrants into cities, and because a lot of them don’t enter secure formal jobs, a sharper class structure forms between industrial elites, a growing urban middle class, and a precarious urban labor force. So class formation isn’t just about growth creating wealth — it’s about who can actually access the gains from urbanization.
exchanges.tutor: That's an excellent insight — and it's exactly the kind of argument that makes a reflection piece compelling. You now have a main claim, a critique of urban bias, a position on linkages, and a nuanced point about class formation. I'd encourage you to start drafting. How are you thinking about organizing these ideas across your 700–800 words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent conceptual leap — connecting migration, labor market segmentation, and unequal access to urbanization gains into a coherent argument about class formation. This is strong thinking. We've now covered the core ideas for all three parts of the prompt. Given that we're about 5 exchanges in and the student has developed solid ideas, I should push them toward integrating this into their written reflection, since the rules suggest prompting synthesis when good ideas have accumulated.

exchanges.student: I think maybe intro with my main claim, then one paragraph on urban bias, one on why linkages matter more than choosing urban vs rural, and one on industrial growth/class formation. I’m not totally sure if that’s too simple though, or if I need a counterpoint in there.
exchanges.tutor: What do you think a counterpoint would add? Who might push back on your main claim, and would addressing that make your argument stronger or just longer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid structure in mind and is now questioning whether they need a counterargument. This is a good critical instinct worth developing. Rather than telling them yes or no, I should ask them to think about what a counterargument would do for their piece — this helps them decide for themselves whether it strengthens their argument.

exchanges.student: I think a counterpoint could be that concentrating investment in cities is the fastest way to generate national growth, especially when resources are limited. That probably would make my argument stronger if I keep it short, because then I can say urban investment matters but it shouldn’t come at the expense of rural livelihoods and balanced linkages.
exchanges.tutor: That works well — it sharpens your argument rather than muddying it. You've got everything you need: a claim, a critique, a position on linkages, a point on class formation, and a counterargument. It's time to start drafting. Give it a go and come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a relevant counterargument and explained how engaging it strengthens rather than dilutes their thesis. This is good argumentative thinking. They now have all the components of a well-structured reflection. I should affirm this and prompt them to start writing, since we've been in planning mode long enough and the rules suggest moving toward a written attempt after sustained good discussion.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I think I can start now. If I get stuck, I’ll probably need help making the intro sound less vague.
exchanges.tutor: Just a heads up — I won't edit or suggest wording for your intro, that part is yours. But if you bring a draft, I can ask questions that help you see where it's working or where it needs more precision. Good luck — go write!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is hinting that they may want help writing or polishing their intro, which falls outside my role as a tutor. I should set a clear boundary on that now — I won't write or suggest language for them — but encourage them to come back with a draft attempt so we can work through it together using questions.

exchanges.student: Got it. I’ll draft it first, and if it feels fuzzy, I’ll bring back the main idea and see if I’m actually answering all parts of the prompt.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right approach. See you when you have a draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has internalized a good self-checking strategy and is ready to write independently. A brief, encouraging closing response is appropriate here. No need to add more scaffolding.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the critique of urban bias, the linkages argument, the class formation mechanism — were drawn out of the student through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. The tutor consistently built on student progress, kept responses concise and interactive, maintained a supportive tone, and explicitly declined to provide wording help at the end, reinforcing the formative framing. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready content; every substantive claim originated with the student. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on each student response before advancing, and used diagnostic questions rather than method dumps. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., pointing out the underdeveloped mechanism in Turn 4, asking the student to connect two ideas in Turn 5). Dialogue quality was strong: the conversation progressed without repetition or drift, and the assignment was kept in focus throughout. Communication was bite-sized and appropriately toned, with the tutor's Turn 9 refusal to edit wording being a particularly clear signal of formative rather than evaluative framing.
